claimed_contribution: It specifies reference price effects in brand choice with range-frequency theory, providing a more complete distribution-sensitive alternative to single-point reference price measures.
It shows that the value of range-frequency modeling rises with higher second and third moments of the reference price distribution, especially in volatile pricing environments.
It identifies moderators of range versus frequency weighting, showing stronger range effects for coupon users and stronger frequency effects under price-trend exposure.
context: Empirical tests use supermarket scanner panel purchases in three packaged-goods categories: 32-ounce ketchup, one-pound stick margarine, and 6.5-ounce light tuna. The data come from ACNielsen, covering nine stores in two markets over 123 weeks in a high-low promotional pricing environment.
design_and_data: The authors estimate mixed multinomial logit brand choice models with household-level random coefficients, including price, display, feature, loyalty, and alternative price-judgment terms. For each category, they compare baseline stimulus-based and memory-based reference price formulations against range-frequency formulations, and also estimate models containing both terms; fit is evaluated with estimation-sample BIC, validation-sample log-likelihoods, and likelihood-ratio tests for nested models. Data are split into 52-week initialization, 44-week estimation, and 27-week validation samples, with households required to have at least 10 category purchases across the 123 weeks. The memory-based range-frequency window is selected empirically by category (4 months for margarine, 7 for ketchup, 10 for tuna).
main_findings: Range-frequency price judgment coefficients are negative and significant, and adding them improves fit over baseline single-point reference price models in both stimulus-based and memory-based choice contexts across categories.
The effect of range-frequency judgments on choice is stronger when the reference price distribution has variance and when it is more skewed.
Frequency receives more weight when households are exposed to price trends, whereas range receives more weight for coupon users.
one_line_summary: Using ACNielsen scanner panel data, the paper embeds range-frequency theory in mixed logit brand choice models and shows that distribution-sensitive price judgments outperform single-point reference price measures.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior reference price choice models that rely on central tendency or single-point summaries by incorporating range and rank information from price distributions. extends laboratory evidence on range-frequency effects in price perception to actual household brand choices in scanner panel data.
research_gap: Brand choice models usually operationalize reference price with a single-point summary, typically central tendency, which captures first-moment effects but cannot represent distributional effects such as range, modality, and skew documented in behavioral research. The paper addresses how to incorporate these distributional effects into scanner-data brand choice models.
research_question: Can consumer price judgments in brand choice be modeled more accurately by range-frequency theory than by standard single-point reference price formulations in both stimulus-based and memory-based contexts? The paper also asks when range-frequency judgments matter most, such as under higher variance and skew in reference price distributions, and what shifts the range-versus-frequency weighting in those judgments.
